content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Time to decide2026 is shaping up to be a year of truth for AI. Investment in AI since 2013 stands at $2 trillion, according to a Reuters report. That does not include $1 trillion in AI-related debt now coming online. Most of the investment is in infrastructure, not in doing AI research and development. Investors are assuming that the few companies in the race got the science right. But did they? Expectation is leading evidence by a large margin. The investment in the Apollo moonshot program was $300 billion in today’s dollars. The ROI was at least an order of magnitude larger. What is driving the investment world to believe the current AI moonshot will create a return that justifies the investment?
The same words used in the title of this article were on the cover of The Economist on May 9th, 2015. A quote from that issue rings true today:
Today’s AI produces the semblance of intelligence through brute number-crunching force, without any great interest in approximating how minds equip humans with autonomy, interests and desires. Computers do not yet have anything approaching the wide, fluid ability to infer, judge and decide that is associated with intelligence in the conventional human sense.
The Economist piece was published in the same year that OpenAI was formed (December 11, 2015). It contained a warning about a situation that confronts us today. The challenge is the framing of AI as a superintelligence learned from data in a single observer model. The training process for GPTs is extremely lengthy, highly power consumptive, and is based on the assumption that all intelligence can be captured in a computational system learning from historical data. Thus, massive data centers work tirelessly as language model factories as if we are in some new industrial revolution. What if it doesn’t work as expected?
A June 2026 article from DeepMind, titled “Solipsistic Superintelligence is Unlikely to be Cooperative,” concludes that the binding constraint on beneficial AI is shifting from capability to coexistence, and that cooperation among intelligences is not a task to be solved by a single system but an equilibrium-selection process that must be designed for. What that means is that there is growing evidence that the expectation of creating a super intelligence from our current methods will not work.
The corporate world is now investing heavily in AI, expecting it is essential to their future. Here as well there is no evidence to date that the promise will result in real returns. Corporate boards increasingly face an expectation vs. evidence gap.
I write this from a vantage point that few have. I was CEO of the first AI company to go public in the first wave of AI and I lived through the onset of an AI winter in the late 80s. The reason for the winter was not that the expectations of return were not met. Indeed, important innovations resulted from first-wave AI. The problem was that the costs exceeded the value delivered. That chill I felt then is in the air today.
I was also CEO of one of the first three companies to go public at the onset of the ecommerce wave, Connect Inc. Connect went public in 1996, followed by multiple companies in the category. The dot-com bubble burst in 2000. Expectations in the ecommerce market had exceeded the value delivered. In both cases it was not the technology or the concept. It was all driven by a known issue. Investors tend to swarm and follow each other causing over-anticipation. In the short term, value collapses. Over time, value is created. AI recovered from the AI Winter. Ecommerce is doing well.
What is common to these cycles is a correction followed by growth that builds over time to a positive long term outcome. There will be a positive outcome to the AI story as well. What is needed? Shifting the perspective from a solipsistic AI (as an all-knowing oracle) to a collaborative superintelligence of humans and machines working, learning and acting together. This approach builds on the value of generative AI by adding a critical new layer of coordination, collaboration, and synthesis — the missing intelligence layer to bring it all together.
While generative AI is getting all the airtime, substantial progress has been made building this missing cooperative intelligence layer. In April 2015, the year the Economist article was published, I cofounded a company to focus on the application of AI to collective intelligence, with the question:
How can people and computers be connected so that collectively they act more intelligently than any person, group, or computer has ever done before?
This is the vision of MIT’s Center for Collective Intelligence. We embraced it as our vision. It worked!
We are not alone. Others have been developing alternative approaches to AI, including work on the Free Energy Principle and Active Inference. These approaches have different training methods with radically different infrastructure requirements. In general, they are more deeply grounded in an understanding of intelligence than “brute number-crunching” force. As a result, energy consumption is vastly reduced. Progress in federated learning, collective intelligence, and neuro-symbolic methods are all opening viable alternative futures for AI. It is only in the public narrative that AI has a singular definition linked to GPTs.
Solipsism is defined as being extremely self-centered or holding the philosophical belief that your own mind is the only thing that is guaranteed to exist. The current race to AGI is fundamentally on the wrong track. Not only does it have the potential to be destructive to human potential, corporate culture and civil society. It is also accelerating the impact of climate change.
Z and Alpha generations understand this and have become outspoken about these concerns. They are asking the right questions.
The power of collective intelligence and cooperative intelligence is now emerging as the way forward. This is a fundamental reframing from single observer learning to multi-observer learning — federated learning — the essence of true superintelligence. Collective intelligence has served us well throughout history. It is the process that led to modern science, underpins our judicial system, and is essential to deliberative democracy. As the foundation of an emerging AI architecture, its true potential is becoming clear.
In a recent paper co-authored with Scott Page (University of Michigan), Martin Reeves (BCG Henderson Institute) and Alex Pentland (MIT/Stanford), we proposed a framework for how humans and AI agents reason together at scale to create a new form of AI called Generative Collective Intelligence. This architectural approach is critical to guide automated knowledge acquisition as we move into an Agentic AI world. Human intelligence and Artificial Intelligence working cooperatively as participants in a process of Intelligence Amplification is the future.
Economically, this perspective change in AI shifts the benefit of AI from labor replacement to acceleration of innovation and agility in business transformation.
Consider the following side-by-side comparison: The concept of AI-driven labor replacement requires a substantial leap of faith that AI can replace people — including innate human context, judgement and organizational knowledge. A study at Carnegie Mellon benchmarking LLM Agents found that agents were only reliably able to complete 30% of tasks autonomously:
“We test baseline agents powered by both closed API-based and open-weights language models LMs, and find that the most competitive agent can complete 30% of tasks autonomously. “
Even if AI Agent task performance radically improved, there is unbounded uncertainty in the societal costs of a 30% reduction in the labor force. Risk is layered on risk.
On the other hand, if we seek return on investment by empowering people with new AI tools that empower human collaboration to accelerate innovation and business transformation, the outlook shifts dramatically. This approach could lead to the creation of entirely new technologies and economic opportunities. The result can be breakthrough solutions to environmental impacts, as well as to some of our most challenging problems in healthcare, energy production and storage and job creation. A new entrepreneurial ecosystem can be powered by a cooperative superintelligence that integrates the insights and creative potential of humans and AI collaborating.
The untapped opportunity is clear, and it is massive. We must shift from AI as a “solipsistic superintelligence” to a cooperative superintelligence that synthesizes the intelligence of humans and AI into a Generative Collective Intelligence (GCI). GCI provides the trust layer that will unleash the true potential of AI by supercharging the most powerful form of intelligence: human collective intelligence. The technology exists. The time to scale is now.
